title: Gastroenterology
summary:
**Hepatology & portal HTN** — Haemochromatosis (AR, HFE, transferrin sat > ferritin, venesection; only cardiomyopathy reversible). Wilson (**AR ATP7B**, **low caeruloplasmin & total serum copper**, KF rings). PBC (F, **pruritus**, **IgM**, AMA-M2, **interlobular bile ducts**, **Sjogren's 80%**, ursodeoxycholic acid). PSC (**intra & extra hepatic ducts**, **UC**, **HIV**, MRCP beaded, p-ANCA, cholangiocarcinoma 10%). Cirrhosis/ascites — **spironolactone**; SBP prophylaxis **Cipro** if protein 15g/L; HRS terlipressin (**splanchnic vasoconstriction**). SAAG >11 = portal HTN/transudate. HCC — **MC cause in UK is Hep C**. Autoimmune hepatitis — **IgG elevated / smooth muscle antibodies**.

**Upper GI/bleeding** — Achalasia (solids+liquids, manometry, bird's beak, balloon dilation). **ZES: fasting gastrin + secretin stimulation**. UGIB — high urea; Glasgow-Blatchford then Rockall. Variceal — **Terlipressin**, prophylactic ABx **reduce mortality**, **band ligation**, **TIPSS**. Barrett's = adenocarcinoma; **EUS for mural invasion**. Eosinophilic oesophagitis — **Fluticasone**.

**Lower GI/malignancy** — Angiodysplasia (AS, APC). Colon Ca (APC, p53, K-ras; HNPCC MC inherited). Peutz-Jeghers (**AD hamartomatous polyps**). **Anal cancer HPV16**. Villous adenoma triad. **Co-Danthramer palliative only (carcinogenic)**.

**IBD** — UC (mucosa, rectum, crypt abscesses, flare on smoking cessation, Truelove-Witt). Crohn's (**small bowel enema: Kantor's string sign, rose thorn ulcers**; azathioprine/mercaptopurine maintain, check TPMT).

**Malabsorption/nutrition** — Coeliac (HLA-DQ2, TTG, check **IgA — false positive TTG**). Whipple's (**PAS granules**, co-trimoxazole 1yr). **SIBO — hydrogen breath test, rifaximin**. Refeeding (hypophosphataemia). MUST vs Waterlow.

**Pancreatic/biliary** — Acute pancreatitis GET SMASHED (**hypertriglyceridaemia**, **azathioprine/didanosine**; **severity by hypocalcaemia or CRP**). Courvoisier. Double duct = pancreatic cancer. **I cells -> CCK**, **biliary colic = colicky RUQ after fatty meal**.

**Infections/hormones** — C. diff (**ingestion of spores**; oral vanc -> fidaxomicin; life-threatening **oral vanc + IV metronidazole**). Hydatid (**surgical excision + albendazole**). GI hormones: I/CCK, **S/secretin**, **D/somatostatin**, G/gastrin. PPI SE: **hyponatraemia, hypomagnesaemia, osteoporosis, microscopic colitis**.
flags: Haemochromatosis: 'Common in caucasian populatio' appears truncated in the source.
Ascites: 'SAAG calculated by Albumin in ascitic fluid - Albumin in serum' should be verified before studying (rewording risks changing meaning).
PBC: '10% have RUQ' appears incomplete in the source.
Liver cirrhosis: 'Transient Elastography (Fibroscan) s the test...' contains a typo/unclear wording.
C. diff: 'First line is oral vancomycin for 1- days' has an incomplete duration.
Acute pancreatitis: 'Amylase is one useful for diagnosis' is unclear source wording.

UC section: the 'First line according to predominant symptom' block appears context-sensitive (reads like IBS management) and was kept as written — verify source context before using it for UC revision.
UGIB table: 'Fresh blood, melon' was preserved exactly but appears unclear (likely 'melena').
UGIB: 'Rockall score after endoscopy: % of rebreeding and mortality' contains typo/unclear wording.
Variceal hemorrhage: 'decrease vatical pressure' appears typo-heavy.
HCC: 'surgical reaction' appears unclear in the source (likely 'resection').
Refeeding syndrome: 'Torsades des pointes secondary to hypomagnesemia is a consequence of referring' appears typo-heavy.
NASH line appears incomplete: 'NASH is a type of NAFLD (non-alcoholic fatty liver'.
Coeliac: 'IgA (which can give a false positive TTG result)' was preserved exactly but should be verified before studying.
Dubin-Johnson: 'Causes a defection...' has typo-heavy wording (likely 'defect').

cards.q: Haemochromatosis: inheritance, gene and chromosome?
cards.a: AR disorder of excess iron absorption; mutations in the **HFE** gene on both copies of chromosome 6.

cards.q: Haemochromatosis: how do you screen, and what test for family members?
cards.a: Screen with transferrin saturation and ferritin (transferrin sat higher than ferritin). For family members: **HFE** genetic testing.

cards.q: Haemochromatosis: which complication is reversible vs irreversible?
cards.a: Reversible: cardiomyopathy and skin pigmentation. Irreversible: liver cirrhosis, DM, hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism, arthropathy.

cards.q: Hereditary haemochromatosis (C282Y homozygous) management?
cards.a: First line: venesection (weekly, then monthly once levels below 50). Second line: Desferrioxamine.

cards.q: Wilson's disease: inheritance and gene?
cards.a: **AR** disorder — ATP7B gene mutation; impaired copper excretion. Young patient with hepatic + neurological features.

cards.q: Wilson's disease: characteristic copper studies?
cards.a: **Low caeruloplasmin and low total serum copper.** Also Kayser-Fleischer rings.

cards.q: PBC: classic autoimmune association (bolded)?
cards.a: **Sjogren's in 80%** (also RA, systemic sclerosis, thyroid disease). Chronic liver disorder in middle-aged females (9:1 F:M).

cards.q: PBC: which ducts are damaged?
cards.a: **Interlobular bile ducts** damaged by chronic inflammation causing progressive cholestasis, may progress to cirrhosis.

cards.q: PBC: the classic symptom and two skin/exam findings that are high-yield?
cards.a: Classic feature: **pruritus**. Also **Xanthelasma** (plus xanthomata, clubbing, hepatosplenomegaly).

cards.q: PBC: characteristic raised immunoglobulin and antibody?
cards.a: Raised serum **IgM**; Anti-mitochondrial Ab M2 subtype in 98% (ANA can be positive; smooth muscle Ab 30%).

cards.q: PBC: first-line management and pruritus treatment?
cards.a: 1st line: Ursodeoxycholic acid (slows progression, improves symptoms). Pruritus: Cholestyramine. Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation. HCC risk raised ~20-fold.

cards.q: PSC: which bile ducts are involved (bolded)?
cards.a: **Intra and extra hepatic bile ducts** — inflammation and fibrosis of unknown aetiology.

cards.q: PSC: key associations (bolded)?
cards.a: **UC** (80% of PSC have UC; 4% of UC have PSC) and **also associated with HIV**.

cards.q: PSC: diagnostic imaging and biopsy appearance?
cards.a: MRCP is diagnostic choice — multiple biliary strictures (beaded appearance). ERCP was old gold standard (higher pancreatitis risk). p-ANCA can be positive; biopsy 'onion skin' fibrosis. Cholangiocarcinoma in 10%, raised colorectal cancer risk.

cards.q: Cirrhotic ascites: which diuretic class is given (bolded)?
cards.a: Patients with **ascites secondary to liver cirrhosis should be given an aldosterone antagonist (Spironolactone).** Add loop diuretic if no response.

cards.q: Cirrhosis scoring systems and severity test?
cards.a: Child-Pugh (severity); MELD (transplant waiting list); Transient Elastography / Fibroscan diagnoses & monitors severity.

cards.q: SAAG >11 g/L indicates what, and name transudative causes?
cards.a: SAAG >11 = portal hypertension (transudate). Liver disease is MC cause (**alcohol, acute liver failure, liver mets**); also right heart failure/constrictive pericarditis, Budd-Chiari, portal vein thrombosis.

cards.q: SAAG <11 g/L indicates what, with causes?
cards.a: SAAG <11 = exudative. Nephrotic syndrome, hypoalbuminaemia/malnutrition, HCC, malignancy (peritoneal), TB peritonitis, acute pancreatitis, bowel obstruction.

cards.q: SBP prophylaxis: when and with what (bolded)?
cards.a: Prophylactic ABx to reduce SBP risk — **Cipro if cirrhosis and ascites with protein of 15 g/L.**

cards.q: Hepatorenal syndrome: treatment and mechanism (bolded)?
cards.a: Terlipressin (vasopressin analogue) — induces **splanchnic vasoconstriction** to reduce portal pressure and improve renal blood flow. Type 1: rapid (<2 weeks, often post-UGIB); Type 2: gradual.

cards.q: SBP: first-line antibiotic and MC organism?
cards.a: IV Cefotaxime (or any 3rd-gen cephalosporin). MC organism: E. coli.

cards.q: HCC: most common cause in the UK (bolded)?
cards.a: **MC cause of HCC in the UK is Hep C.**

cards.q: Autoimmune hepatitis: key labs (bolded)?
cards.a: **IgG elevated / smooth muscle antibodies.**

cards.q: NAFLD: next investigation and management?
cards.a: Next step: enhanced liver fibrosis test. Management: weight loss -> Semaglutide. NASH is a type of NAFLD, associated with metabolic syndrome.

cards.q: Hepatic encephalopathy management (and TIPSS link)?
cards.a: Lactulose and Rifaximin. Also a common complication after a TIPSS procedure. Alcoholic hepatitis: corticosteroids.

cards.q: Factor VIII in liver disease — increased or decreased, and why?
cards.a: Increased, due to reduced clearance by the dysfunctional liver (vitamin-K-dependent factors II, VII, IX are decreased).

cards.q: Liver biopsy contraindications?
cards.a: Deranged clotting (INR >1.4), low platelets (<60), anaemia, extrahepatic biliary obstruction, hydatid cyst, haemangioma, uncooperative patient, ascites.

cards.q: Achalasia: dysphagia pattern, investigations, treatment?
cards.a: Dysphagia to BOTH solids and liquids. Ix: oesophageal manometry (excessive tone), barium swallow (bird's beak), fluid level on CXR. Treatment: balloon dilation.

cards.q: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome: diagnostic test (bolded)?
cards.a: **Fasting gastrin levels with secretin stimulation test.** Excess gastrin from a neuroendocrine (gastrin-secreting) tumour.

cards.q: ZES: most common site, and syndrome association?
cards.a: MC site: first part of duodenum (contains G cells). 30% are part of MEN I. Features: diarrhoea, malabsorption, multiple gastroduodenal ulcers.

cards.q: UGIB: which blood result correlates with an upper source?
cards.a: High urea correlates with UGIB (e.g. variceal bleeds). Presents as haematemesis (coffee ground) and melena.

cards.q: UGIB risk scores: which before vs after endoscopy?
cards.a: Glasgow-Blatchford score for OPAT vs inpatient (pre-endoscopy); Rockall score after endoscopy (rebleeding % and mortality). Endoscopy within 24h (immediately if severe).

cards.q: Non-variceal UGIB: transfusion/clotting thresholds and drug?
cards.a: Platelets if <50 and bleeding; FFP if fibrinogen <1 g/L or PT/APTT >1.5x; prothrombin complex concentrate if on warfarin and bleeding; PPIs for non-variceal bleeding. Surgery if persistent despite repeated endoscopic therapy.

cards.q: Variceal haemorrhage: the four bolded high-priority points?
cards.a: **Terlipressin**; prophylactic ABx to **reduce mortality**; endoscopic variceal **band ligation**; **TIPSS** (if all else fails).

cards.q: Terlipressin in variceal bleeding: mechanism and side effects?
cards.a: Acts on V1 receptors -> splanchnic vasoconstriction -> decreases portal blood flow/pressure. S/E: hypertension, hyponatraemia, mesenteric ischaemia. Sengstaken-Blakemore tube if uncontrolled.

cards.q: Variceal bleeding prophylaxis?
cards.a: Non-selective beta-blockers (Carvedilol lowers hepatic venous pressure gradient; propranolol in decompensated cirrhosis) and endoscopic variceal band ligation (every 2 weeks until eradication).

cards.q: Oesophageal/gastric cancer: what assesses mural invasion (bolded)?
cards.a: **Endoscopic ultrasound is used to assess mural invasion.**

cards.q: Barrett's oesophagus: pathology and management?
cards.a: Metaplasia of lower oesophageal mucosa to columnar epithelium (long-standing GORD -> Barrett's -> adenocarcinoma, signet ring cells). High-dose PPIs + endoscopic surveillance. Gold standard reflux test: 24h oesophageal pH monitoring. Oesophageal cancer is NOT associated with H. pylori.

cards.q: Boerhaave's syndrome: what and investigation?
cards.a: Spontaneous oesophageal rupture; subcutaneous emphysema (palpable crepitus). CT scan with contrast is a must.

cards.q: Plummer-Vinson syndrome triad?
cards.a: IDA + dysphagia (oesophageal webs) + atrophic glossitis (low Hb and MCV). Also called Paterson-Brown-Kelly.

cards.q: Eosinophilic oesophagitis: key management (bolded)?
cards.a: **Fluticasone** (swallowed topical corticosteroids); also PPI and six-food elimination diet.

cards.q: H. pylori testing: which meds interfere and washout periods?
cards.a: ABx stopped 4 weeks before; PPIs/Ranitidine stopped within 2 weeks; Gaviscon/cOCP have no relation to 13C-UBT. MALT lymphoma treated by H. pylori eradication.

cards.q: Angiodysplasia: key association and management ladder?
cards.a: GIT vascular deformity -> IDA; elderly; associated with aortic stenosis. Active bleed -> APC (argon plasma coagulation); unstable -> mesenteric angiography + embolisation; massive -> surgery; recurrent/less severe -> antifibrinolytics (TXA)/hormonal.

cards.q: Colon cancer: sporadic mutation pathway and commonest inherited type?
cards.a: Sporadic: allelic loss of APC, p53 deletion, DCC deletion, K-ras activation. MC inherited colorectal Ca: HNPCC. Associated with endometrial cancer.

cards.q: Peutz-Jeghers syndrome: inheritance and hallmark (bolded)?
cards.a: **AD disease with development of non-cancerous hamartomatous polyps in the GI tract.** Hyperpigmented macules on lips/oral mucosa, IDA, intestinal obstruction; 50% die from cancer by 60.

cards.q: Anal cancer: key association (bolded)?
cards.a: **Anal cancer is strongly associated with HPV16.**

cards.q: Villous adenoma: classic triad?
cards.a: Chronic diarrhoea, hypokalaemia, and dehydration.

cards.q: Acute anal fissure (<1 week): management?
cards.a: Bulk-forming laxatives and diet advice; if no use, topical glyceryl trinitrate.

cards.q: Laxative classes with examples?
cards.a: Osmotic: Lactulose, Macrogols, rectal phosphates. Stimulant: Senna, Docusate, Bisacodyl, Glycerol, Co-Danthramer. Bulk-forming: Ispaghula husk, Methylcellulose. Faecal softener: Arachis oil.

cards.q: Co-Danthramer: key prescribing trap (bolded)?
cards.a: **Co-Danthramer is only prescribed to palliative patients** due to its **carcinogenic potential** (for severe opioid-induced constipation not responding). Pigment-laden macrophages suggest laxative abuse.

cards.q: Ulcerative colitis: layer, site, histology, and flare trigger?
cards.a: Mucosal layer only; MC site rectum; goblet cell depletion; crypt abscesses. A flare-up can be triggered by smoking cessation.

cards.q: UC: Truelove and Witts severity by stools/day?
cards.a: <4 = mild: oral aminosalicylate. 4-6 = moderate: topical rectal aminosalicylates. >6 (+pyrexia, tachycardia, anaemia, raised inflammatory markers) = severe: IV steroids.

cards.q: Crohn's disease: small bowel enema findings (bolded)?
cards.a: **Small bowel enema: Kantor's string sign, rose thorn ulcers.** Colonoscopy is investigation of choice (deep ulcers); non-caseating granulomas; CRP correlates with activity.

cards.q: Crohn's: inducing vs maintaining remission?
cards.a: Remission: glucocorticoids (or budesonide); 5-ASA (Mesalazine) 2nd line; Infliximab if refractory; metronidazole if isolated peri-anal; smoking cessation. Maintain: Azathioprine/Mercaptopurine first-line (check TPMT first; MTX 2nd line).

cards.q: Why check TPMT before azathioprine/mercaptopurine?
cards.a: TPMT deficiency is genetic — if deficient, severe side effects occur; if below normal but not deficient, give lower doses.

cards.q: Sulfasalazine: uses and side effects?
cards.a: Used in IBD and RA. S/E: male infertility, skin rashes, diarrhoea, visual disturbances.

cards.q: Malabsorption: classic features and the three cause categories?
cards.a: Diarrhoea, steatorrhoea, weight loss. Intestinal (coeliac, Crohn's, tropical sprue, Whipple's, giardiasis, lactase insufficiency), pancreatic (chronic pancreatitis, CF, pancreatic cancer), biliary (obstruction, PBC).

cards.q: Coeliac disease: why must you also check IgA (bolded)?
cards.a: Association between coeliac and **IgA (which can give a false positive TTG result).** Coeliac: HLA-DQ2, anaemia, low ferritin; investigation TTG. Hyposplenism -> needs pneumococcal vaccine.

cards.q: Whipple's disease: hallmark biopsy finding (bolded)?
cards.a: Jejunal biopsy — macrophages containing **PAS granules.** Bacterial infection by Tropheryma whipplei; middle-aged men, HLA-B27; joint pain, diarrhoea/malabsorption; CT mesenteric lymphadenopathy. Treat: oral co-trimoxazole for a year.

cards.q: Small bowel bacterial overgrowth (SIBO): first-line test and treatment (bolded)?
cards.a: **Hydrogen breath test (first-line test).** Treatment: rifaximin. Villous atrophy associations: tropical sprue, coeliac, Whipple's, hypogammaglobulinaemia, lymphomas.

cards.q: Refeeding syndrome: hallmark electrolyte changes and prevention?
cards.a: Hallmark hypophosphataemia (also hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia). Prevention: provide only 50% of normal energy/protein requirements for 2 days. MUST screens malnutrition; Waterlow for pressure sores.

cards.q: Bariatric surgery: which procedure for which BMI, and malabsorption?
cards.a: Laparoscopic adjustable gastric banding for BMI 30-39; biliopancreatic diversion with duodenal switch for BMI >60 (most likely to cause malabsorption). PEG not recommended in advanced dementia.

cards.q: Amylase: what does it break down (bolded)?
cards.a: **Amylase breaks down starch into sugars.** (Brush border: Maltase -> glucose+glucose; Sucrase -> glucose+fructose; Lactase -> glucose+galactose.)

cards.q: Acute pancreatitis: causes (GET SMASHED) with bolded high-yield?
cards.a: Gallstones, Ethanol, Trauma, Steroids, Mumps, Autoimmune/Ascaris, Scorpion venom, **Hypertriglyceridaemia**/hypercalcaemia, ERCP, Drugs. Pregnancy pancreatitis most likely due to gallstones.

cards.q: Acute pancreatitis: which drugs are bolded causes, and how is severity assessed (bolded)?
cards.a: Drugs: **Azathioprine**, **Didanosine** (also Mesalazine, Bendroflumethiazide, Furosemide, Pentamidine, Sodium Valproate). **Severity assessed by hypocalcaemia or CRP.**

cards.q: Pancreatic pseudocyst: timing and management?
cards.a: 2-4 weeks post-pancreatitis; 75% with amylase elevation. Ix: CT, ERCP, MRI/EUS. Observe 12 weeks (50% self-resolve); treat with endoscopic/surgical cystogastrostomy/aspiration. Pancreatic abscess (infected pseudocyst) -> drainage.

cards.q: Chronic pancreatitis: commonest cause and exocrine test?
cards.a: 80% due to alcohol excess. Abdominal pain, bulky stools, weight loss. Faecal elastase tests exocrine function; Ix CT abdomen.

cards.q: Biliary colic: which cell produces CCK and the classic pain (bolded)?
cards.a: **I cells in upper small intestine produce CCK.** **Colicky RUQ pain after eating a fatty meal (biliary colic).**

cards.q: Courvoisier's sign and double duct dilatation — significance?
cards.a: Palpable RUQ mass (gallbladder) = Courvoisier's sign, seen in cholangiocarcinoma if painless jaundice. Double duct dilatation (CBD & pancreatic) = pancreatic cancer.

cards.q: C. difficile: transmission and life-threatening management (bolded)?
cards.a: Transmission via faecal-oral route by **ingestion of spores.** Life-threatening line: **oral vancomycin + IV metronidazole.** First line oral vancomycin, 2nd line oral fidaxomicin.

cards.q: C. difficile: risk factors and recurrence management?
cards.a: RF: clindamycin (classic), 2nd/3rd-gen cephalosporins, PPIs. Recurrence <12 weeks -> fidaxomicin; >12 weeks -> vancomycin or fidaxomicin; FMT for 2+ episodes; Bezlotoxumab prevention. Isolate until diarrhoea-free 48h.

cards.q: Hydatid cyst (E. granulosus): management (bolded)?
cards.a: **Surgical excision** with **Albendazole pre- and post-operatively to reduce recurrence.**

cards.q: GI hormone cells: I, S, D, G — hormones and actions (bolded)?
cards.a: **I cells -> CCK**; **S cells -> Secretin** (**increases bicarbonate-rich fluid** from pancreas/hepatic duct); **D cells -> Somatostatin** (inhibits gastrin); G cells (antrum) -> Gastrin (increases HCl from parietal cells, gastric motility).

cards.q: Carcinoid syndrome: features and investigation?
cards.a: Flushing (first symptom), diarrhoea, bronchospasm, tricuspid stenosis (soft mid-diastolic murmur), pellagra (vit B3/niacin deficiency). Urinary 5-HIAA. Affects the right side of the heart (pulmonary stenosis, tricuspid insufficiency).

cards.q: PPI side effects (bolded high-yield)?
cards.a: **Hyponatraemia** and **hypomagnesaemia**; **osteoporosis** (raised fracture risk); **microscopic** colitis (diagnosed by colonoscopy); C. diff.
